coredata.dc:title: The graphene quantum dots encased in the molecularly imprinted polymer as a new fluorescent nanosensor for the detection of biotin
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammadnejad, Parizad
Hosseini, Seyed Mohamadreza Milani
Sohrabi, Beheshteh
coredata.dc:description: In aqueous solution, a non-toxic fluorescent nanosensor incorporating graphene quantum dots encased in molecularly imprinted polymer (GQDs-encased in MIP) is manufactured through a straightforward sol-gel process. During the polymerization process, the functional monomer 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and the cross-linker tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were utilized to bind the biotin in a polymer network. The resulting GQDs@MIP nanocomposite outperformed the similar non-imprinted polymer (GQDs-encased in NIP) in terms of biotin selectivity. Under ideal conditions, the produced GQD-encased in MIP are employed to detect biotin by quenching their fluorescence caused by the target analyte via photo induced electron transfer (PET). The quenching curves of each GQDs-encased in polymer were fitted with the Stern-Volmer-type equation, and GQDs-encased in MIP had a broader linear range and a lower limit of detection than GQDs-encased in NIP. GQDs-encased in MIP fluorescence response is linear with respect to biotin concentration over a wide linear range of at least 0.4 μmol L − 1 to 6.7 μmol L − 1. The detection limit for biotin determination was 315 nmol L − 1. The suggested GQDs-encased in MIP is promising for the measurement of trace biotin in human serum samples because to its non-toxicity, simplicity, and low cost, as well as its strong analytical performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Biotin
Fluorescence sensor

Preparation and characterization of GQDs– encased in MIP
Factors affecting the nanosensor's luminescence in the presence of the target molecule
Biotin measurement with GQDs-encased in MIP / GQDs-encased in NIP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of GQDs-encased in MIP synthesis.
FT-IR spectra of GQDs (A), APTES-capped GQDs (B) and GQDs-encased in MIP (C).
TEM image of the GQDs (A) and GQDs-encased in MIP (B).

The EDX image of GQDs-encased in MIP (A–E) displays the EDS analysis results of GQDs-encased in MIP, (F) confirms that the synthesis of MIP on the surface of GQDs, (G, H) shows the percentage of constituent elements presented in the GQDs-encased in MIP.
The height analyses of the GQDs as shown in AFM image.
The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GQDs.
The fluorescence emission peak of GQDs (red) and GQDs-APTES (black).
The fluorescence intensities of non-imprinted composite (black curve) and composite after (blue curve) and before (purple curve) the template was removed.

Fluorescence spectra of GQDs-encased in MIP (red curve) before and (black curve) after template removed, GQDs-encased in MIP with an increase of 3 μM of biotin (green curve). The GQDs-encased in MIP concentration was 0.05 mg.mL−1 (A), and the fluorescence spectra of GQDs-encased in MIP and NIP (B).
The pH-dependent fluorescence intensity changes of GQDs-encased in MIP by biotin (5 μM).

Fluorescence emission spectra of A) GQDs-encased in MIP and B) GQDs-encased in NIP with the addition of different concentrations of biotin (0–10 µΜ) in PBS buffer (pH=7.4) and C) Standard curve, related Stern-Volmer plots from GQDs-encased in MIP and NIP with biotin. The concentration of GQDs-encased in MIP and GQDs -encased in NIP was 0.050 ppm.

The stability of sensor GQDs remains constant for four months, suggesting no visible discernible aggregate and no appreciable decrease in the FL intensity. GQDs-encased in MIP (A), GQDs-encased in NIP (B).

Selective adsorption of biotin, ascorbic acid, sucrose, uric acid and glucose (2 μM) by GQDs-encased in MIP and GQDs-encased in NIP at pH 7.4 (PBS buffer), the concentration of GQDs encased in MIP and GQDs-encased in NIP was 0.05 ppm.
The stability investigation of the GQDs-encased in MIP toward time.
A comparison of detection limits as regards biotin concentrations was taken from recent publications.

row.entry: Graphene quantum dots embedded silica molecular imprinted polymer (GQDs-embedded SMIP)

graphene quantum dots encased in molecularly imprinted polymer (GQDs-encased in MIP)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results for the determination of biotin in human serum samples.
Tolerable concentration of interfering species in fluorescence quenching of GQDs-encased in MIP in the presence of biotin.

head.ce:title: The graphene quantum dots encased in the molecularly imprinted polymer as a new fluorescent nanosensor for the detection of biotin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Real Samples Analysis, Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology, P.O. Box 1684613114, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Real Samples Analysis
Iran University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aResearch Laboratory of Real Samples Analysis, Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology, P.O. Box 1684613114, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Surface Chemistry Research Laboratory, Iran University of Science and Technology, Tehran 16846-13114, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Iran University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Chemistry, Surface Chemistry Research Laboratory, Iran University of Science and Technology, Tehran 16846-13114, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In aqueous solution, a non-toxic fluorescent nanosensor incorporating graphene quantum dots encased in molecularly imprinted polymer (GQDs-encased in MIP) is manufactured through a straightforward sol-gel process. During the polymerization process, the functional monomer 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and the cross-linker tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were utilized to bind the biotin in a polymer network. The resulting GQDs@MIP nanocomposite outperformed the similar non-imprinted polymer (GQDs-encased in NIP) in terms of biotin selectivity. Under ideal conditions, the produced GQD-encased in MIP are employed to detect biotin by quenching their fluorescence caused by the target analyte via photo induced electron transfer (PET). The quenching curves of each GQDs-encased in polymer were fitted with the Stern-Volmer-type equation, and GQDs-encased in MIP had a broader linear range and a lower limit of detection than GQDs-encased in NIP. GQDs-encased in MIP fluorescence response is linear with respect to biotin concentration over a wide linear range of at least 0.4 μmol L − 1 to 6.7 μmol L − 1. The detection limit for biotin determination was 315 nmol L − 1. The suggested GQDs-encased in MIP is promising for the measurement of trace biotin in human serum samples because to its non-toxicity, simplicity, and low cost, as well as its strong analytical performance.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sensors and biosensors have captivated the attention of researchers in recent years in order to recognize analytes because one of the important targets for an analytical chemist is the extension of techniques that allow the fast, selective, and sensitive determination of the purpose analyte [1–3]. Among the manufactured sensors, the one using fluorescence methods has been outstandingly used in the examination of diverse substances, as fluorescence methods provide significant qualities such as easy detection, proper sensitivity, and the possibility for high throughput analysis [4–8]. Among fluorescent approaches, quantum dots (QDs) have received a lot of attention [9]. The toxicity and potential environmental risk of the heavy metals employed in conventional metal quantum dots, such as Cd, limit their applicability. As a result, the expansion of eco-friendly replacements is quite beneficial. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are metal-free compounds, have recently demonstrated a great potential for substituting regular metal QDs. Graphene is usually referred to as a "marvel substance" because of its huge surface area, rapid electron transfer velocity, and high electrical conductivity [10,11]. However, graphene has a strong hydrophobic character, which may act as an inhibitor for analyte recapture in aqueous solutions, probably due to the interfacial resistance. Furthermore, GQDs are graphene sheets with a hybrid structure of sp2 and sp3 that are thinner than 100 nm in size [12,13]. Because of their small dimensions, these compounds are classified as substituted compounds with large surface area and excellent conductivity [14]. Furthermore, they have endearing properties including excellent photostability and fluorescence activity due to quantum confinement and edge effects [15]. Due to the difficulty in locating GQDs, few publications on GQD-based fluorescence sensors have been published, which can provide both a sensitive signal response and selective detection of an analyte [16]. In the creation of nanosensors, QDs are used in a variety of ways. Nevertheless, one method for enhancing the selectivity of nanosensors prepared from QDs is molecular imprinted polymer technology [17]. Molecular imprinting is a well-established technique for creating molecularly selective holes inside polymer matrices during polymerization [18–22]. The template molecule is present during the polymerization procedure and is held in place by fragile intermolecular interactions such as van der Waals and hydrogen bonding. The template molecule is thus physically imprisoned in the cavities of the cross-linked polymer after polymerization. By removing the template molecule from the polymer, ghost-sites that are extremely selective for the template molecule rebinding in comparison to molecules of comparable shape and size are created [23]. When compared to antibodies, enzymes, or biological molecules, MIPs have the following inherent properties: i) MIP synthesis is simple and inexpensive. ii) MIPs have good physical and chemical stability. iii) MIPs can be used in hostile chemical environments without losing their blinding properties [24]. Biotin (vitamin H) is a water-soluble vitamin that is required for amino acid and carbohydrate metabolism as well as some nucleic acid synthesis. As a result, monitoring biotin levels in food and human serum is critical. Many methods for detecting biotin in foods and biological samples have been published, including immunoassays using monoclonal antibodies [25], microbiology evaluation using Lactobacillus plantarum [26], liquid chromatography coupled with a fluorescence detector [27], HPLC coupled with a new type of chromatographic detector (a corona-charged aerosol detector) [28], and chemometric instrumentation, which uses data from electrochemical and fluorescence measurements. [29]. However, all of these procedures are extremely sensitive, and the use of biological chemicals limited their robustness while increasing the cost of research. Chemical strategies for biotin identification have also been introduced, including spectrophotometric approaches utilizing dye-protein complexes [30], reactions with 4-dimethylaminocinnamaldehyde [31], and oxidation with potassium iodate [27]. Nonetheless, these procedures are neither selective nor sensitive in terms of microbiological approaches. Furthermore, several high-performance liquids chromatographic (HPLC) processes for biotin evaluation have recently been reported [32], including HPLC coupled with an electrochemical detector [33] and fluorescent reactions involving derivatization, including 4-bromomethyl-7-methoxycumarin [34], anthryldiazomethane [35], and 1-pyrenyldiazomethane [36]. Furthermore, these approaches are highly sensitive and selective, with interferences seen in their applications. Furthermore, in the search for simpler ways, it would be preferable to use well-established analytical techniques due to their wide range of applications in many laboratories.

For the first time, surface imprinting and nanotechnology were coupled to construct GQDs-encased in MIP composites in order to detect the material biotin in plasma matrix. Our goal in this work is to develop a fluorescent sensor with specific locations that will allow for the quick, straightforward, and reliable quantitative identification of biotin in human fluids. The proposed sensor is a novel nanosensor that combines MIP and GQDs and exhibits great sensitivity, repeatability, and stability. GQDs incorporated in MIP composite sensors for biotin analysis have never been reported in the literature, to the best of our knowledge.

Double-distilled water (DDW) and analytical-grade reagents without any pretreatment operation or subsequent purification were employed for the preparation of all solutions. Citric acid (CA), 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES), methanol, tetraethoxysilane (TEOS), ethanol, ascorbic acid, ammonium fluoride, sulfuric acid, acetic acid, as well as additional chemical agents, were purchased from Merck (Germany). Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was used during the experiments. Biotin, riboflavin (vitamin B2), and thiamine (vitamin B1) hydrochloride were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA).

UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained with a spectrophotometer UV Mini 1240 Shimadzu (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). On a Shimadzu FTIR-4800S, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopic measurements were made. A Philips CM30 TEM was used to capture the pictures produced by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). A Shimadzu RF-6000 luminescence spectrometer was used to perform the fluorescence measurements.

The method used by Dong et al. to create GQDs was followed, although with a few modifications [37]. In short, 2 g of anhydrous citric acid was placed in a three-necked flask and heated to 200 °C for around 20 min under a nitrogen atmosphere, until the citric acid became an orange liquid. The GQDs were dispersed in 30 ml of anhydrous ethanol and 2 mL of APTES were added. Under reflux circumstances, the solution was heated to 60 °C for 6 h. The mixture was then filtered and centrifuged in ice-cold petroleum ether. GQDs-APTES was produced after washing the mixture twice with petroleum ether. For following investigations, GQDs-APTES were disseminated in anhydrous ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs-encased in MIP and NIP
ce:section.ce:para: A sol-gel copolymerization procedure was used to create MIP-coated GQDs. The template molecules, functional monomers, and cross-linkers were biotin, APTES, and TEOS, respectively. Biotin was mixed in 10 mL of GQDs-APTES ethanol solution (concentration 40 mg mL−1) and agitated for 30 min. The mixture was then injected with 1 mL of APTES and stirred at 500 rpm for 2 h at room temperature. The mixture was then agitated for 6 h with 500 µL of TEOS and 350 µL of ammonium fluoride solution (catalytic solution containing 0.2 g NH4F, 10 mL H2O, and 2.3 mL NH4OH). Finally, the product was centrifuged at 6000 rpm and washed with ethanol to remove unreacted compounds, as well as 10% acidified methanol to remove template molecules, until no biotin was found in the washing solution by the UV spectrophotometer. The GQDs encased in MIP composites were gathered and allowed to dry at 65 °C overnight. Without the use of template molecules, non-imprinted polymer NIP-coated GQD composites were also created in a similar manner.

The excitation wavelength was set to 340 nm, the maximum emission peak was set to 450 nm, and both the emission and excitation gap widths were set to 10 nm for all of the fluorescence (FL) spectra in this experiment. Following the addition and dissolution of an acceptable amount of GQDs-encased in MIP or GQDs-encased in NIP composite into the 5 mL phosphate buffer saline 0.1 M (pH=7.5), the various concentrations of biotin (ranging from 300 nmol L − 1 to 10 µmol L − 1) were added in a stepwise manner. The mixture was then thoroughly mixed, and the spectrofluorometer was used to measure the amount of quenching after each addition. In order to probe into any potential interference effects, ascorbic acid, sucrose, uric acid, urea, and glucose were also assessed in identical conditions.

Human serum samples were collected from mature, healthy applicants at Tehran's Alghadir Hospital. Initially, the serum samples were treated with acetic acid to denature the proteins. Following the precipitation of the plasma proteins, the liquid was vortexed and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to extract the remaining protein. After removing the liquid supernatant, biotin concentrations in serum samples were determined using the proposed approach outlined in Section 2.5, and recovery was obtained by spiking 0.5, 1.0, 3.0, and 5.0 µmol L − 1 biotin into the sample to evaluate the devised method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of GQDs– encased in MIP
ce:section.ce:para: To alter the interaction between the GQDs and the molecularly imprinted silica matrix, the GQDs should be covered in a layer of silica before being imprinted with sol-gel [38]. Three processes are involved in the synthesis of GQD-encased in MIP. First, GQDs must be created. Next, APTES-capped GQDs must be created as fluorescent materials. Finally, the GQD-encased in MIP must be created using a simple sol-gel polymerization in the presence of APTES-capped GQDs, as well as biotin as a template, APTES as a functional monomer, TEOS as a cross-linker, and the ammonium fluoride catalytic solution. The GQD-encased in MIPs were created after the template was removed. The schematic of GQD-encased in MIP synthesis is shown in Fig. 1 .

The FT-IR spectra of GQDs (A), APTES-capped GQDs (B), and GQD-encased in MIP (C) are shown in Fig. 2 . The broad band around 3440 cm−1 is assigned to hydroxyl groups, and the C = O vibrations are seen as a weak peak around 1652 cm−1 and 1250 cm−1, with a peak around 1574 cm−1 due to COO asymmetric stretching vibrations, according to the FT-IR absorption spectrum of GQDs. The presence of an amide group (-CONH-) is demonstrated by the peak at 1680 cm1 that belongs to the CN vibration bond in Fig. 2B. Peaks at 765 cm−1 and 465 cm−1 also indicate the presence of Si-O vibrations and demonstrate that silica is bound to GQDs. The Si-O-C and Si-O-Si asymmetric stretching, which ensures the attachment of the silica portion on the GQDs, are attributed to the strong and wide peaks around 1075 cm−1 and 1125 cm−1 in the FT-IR absorption spectrum of GQDs encased in MIP, respectively. Furthermore, the presence of Si-O vibrations is indicated by absorption peaks at around 760 cm−1 and 460 cm−1. These results show that TEOS and APTES copolymerize on the surface of silica-encased in GQDs.

TEM was used to assess the morphology of the as-prepared biosensor (Fig. 3 ). Fig. 3 shows GQDs (A) and GQDs-encased in MIP (B) particles with size distributions of roughly 2 and 100 nm diameters, respectively, and spherical shape for GQDs. Because of the amorphous polymeric nature of MIP particles, the morphology of GQDs-encased in MIP had uneven forms, as illustrated in Fig. 3(B). EDS analysis was performed on GQDs-encased in MIP in order to identify the elements present in its structure. Fig. 4 (A–F) displays the EDS analysis results of GQDs-encased in MIP, wherein biotin acted as the template molecule. The EDS analysis findings show that GQDs and GQDs encased in MIP structures include O and C elements (Fig. 4 (A, C)). Notably, N and Si elements predominantly originate from MIP, as evident in Fig. 4 (B, D). Furthermore, Fig. 4 (B, E, F) illustrates the presence of N and S elements, which primarily stem from the biotin template molecule. The weight percentage of constituent elements is presented in the Fig. 4 (G, H) spectrum. Upon analysis, it was confirmed that the synthesis of MIP on the surface of GQDs was successful. The surface morphology of the GQDs was investigated by Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis in order to determine their thickness. According to Fig. 5 (A–C) the AFM analysis findings show that thickness of GQDs is 0.035 nm (Fig. 5C).

The aromatic system, the band gap, and the absorption edge of the GQDs were thought to be responsible for the suggested GQDs-encased in MIP biosensor's wide range of UV–vis adsorption bands, which extend from 300 nm to 400 nm (Fig. 6 ). GQDs have functional groups C = O and carboxylate (COO) on their surface. Bands at 350 nanometers may be the result of n→π* transitions including C = O and carboxylate (COO) functional groups on the GQDs.

After the template was removed from the polymer matrix, the fluorescence intensity of GQD-encased in MIP increased. Furthermore, when the composite interacted with biotin molecules, the fluorescence intensity was efficiently quenched, and an energy transfer between GQDs and biotin molecules occurred, which was responsible for the quenching event [40]. As a result, the fluorescence emission intensity of the sensor decreases significantly in the presence of biotin molecules, as illustrated in Fig. 9 . The fluorescence quenching event in this example is caused by a sort of photo-induced electron transfer (PET). PET is an electron transfer method that transfers an excited state electron from the donor (biotin) to the acceptor (GQDs). When a photon excites a molecule, an electron in its ground state orbital might be stimulated to a higher-energy orbital. This excited state creates a vacancy in a ground-state orbital that can be filled by an electron donor. It generates an electron in a high-energy orbital that can be accepted by an electron acceptor. A photo-excited molecule can act as an excellent oxidizing or reducing agent under these conditions. The end consequence of both processes is an electron transferring to a higher energy orbital than the one it is currently in. When operating with semiconductors, this phenomenon is commonly described as a charge-separated electron-hole pair. It is feasible for such molecules to emit common fluorescence in the absence of an electron acceptor or donor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors affecting the nanosensor's luminescence in the presence of the target molecule
ce:section.ce:para: Some parameters, such as pH and response time, that may influence fluorescence-quenching performance must be tuned.

Besides, fluorescence is delicate to pH. Absorption of 5 µM of biotin at several pHs by GQDs-encased in MIP was measured in order to determine the optimal pH for biotin determination (Fig. 10 ). Fig. 10 shows that the most quenching fluorescence has happened at pH= 7.5, and therefore, this pH opted for further investigation.

Tracing the fluorescence intensity of the composite allowed us to assess the suggested sensor's stability over time in the presence of biotin. The results are displayed in Table 1 . The best response time for the determination of 5 µM biotin by GQDs-encased in MIP was 6 min based on the data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biotin measurement with GQDs-encased in MIP / GQDs-encased in NIP
ce:section.ce:para:
By adding biotin (concentrations ranging from 0.3 to 10 µM) under optimum circumstances (10 mM in saline buffer, pH = 7.4, and time mixed = 6 min), the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-encased in MIP/GQDs-encased in NIP was measured. Biotin quenched the fluorescence sensors. The fluorescence spectra of GQDs-encased in MIP/GQDs-encased in NIP, presented in Fig. 11 , reveal that when biotin concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity decreased. The fluorescent intensity of GQDs-encased in MIP considerably decreased as the biotin concentration was raised, despite the fact that both GQDs-encased in MIP and GQDs-encased in NIP displayed fluorescence quenching responses to biotin. When biotin was added, the GQDs-encased in NIP did not change appreciably, however the GQDs-encased in MIP were more sensitive to the presence of biotin. The GQDs-encased in MIP as opposed to the GQDs-encased in NIP have binding sites that are better suited to attracting the template biotin molecule, which results in the sensor's exceptional quench. The Stern-Volmer equation, illustrated in Eq. (1), can be used to characterize the fluorescence quenching performance:
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]

Eq. (1) explains that F0 and F are the sensor's fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of biotin, respectively; the Stern-Volmer quenching constant in the Eq. (1) is Ksv; and [Q] is the biotin concentration [41].

When the biotin concentration was increased from 300 nM to 10 µM, the Stern-Volmer plots of GQD-encased in MIP and GQD-encased in NIP both displayed a linear relationship, as shown in Fig. 9. The linear correlation of the GQD-encased in MIP on biotin concentrations ranging from 0.4 to 6.5 µM was R2 = 0.9909 using the linear regression equation F0/F = 0.1531 [biotin] + 0.9983. With a signal-to-noise ratio of 5, it was discovered that the detection limit was 0.315 µM. The linear correlation R2 =0.9827 on the concentration of biotin in the range of 0.4 to 6.5 µM is shown by the GQD-encased in NIP using the linear regression equation F0/F = 0.0127 [biotin] + 0.995. The detection limit was set at 0.375 µM. The interaction of the COOH group in biotin with GQD-encased in MIP NH2 on the surface of the imprinted polymer induces fluorescence quenching when biotin is added separately to GQD-encased in MIP or GQD-encased in NIP solutions. We propose that this connection is generated by electron transfer from donor (biotin) to acceptor (GQDs-encased in MIP) based on the interaction of banded hydrogen. This electron transfer occurs from the HOMO levels of biotin to the LUMO levels of GQDs, and results in fluorescence quenching. However, due to the effectiveness of molecular imprinting, the amount of biotin bound to GQDs-encased in MIP was greater than that bound to GQDs-encased in NIP. The correlation of NIP (R2 = 0.9823) is lower than that of MIP (R2=0.9909), as demonstrated in (Fig. 10C). We utilized GQDs-encased in MIP as a selective sensor for the quantitative determination of trace quantities of biotin as a result of the aforementioned findings.
Comparison of performance with prior research is necessary to highlight the significance of the current work. In Table 2 , some results of the previous studies are contrasted.

The stability of the sensors GQDs dispersion changing with time is of great importance for practical use. Precipitation has not formed, and there has not been a substantial change in the solution's color, according to the stability of the sensors GQD dispersion over the past four months. The PL spectra in Fig. 12 (A, B) showed no discernible aggregate and no appreciable decrease in the PL intensity.

The standard addition technique was used to obtain a recovery in the range of 96.00 to 104.00% in order to determine the applicability and reliability of the GQDs-encased in MIP fluorescent sensor used for biotin analysis in human serum samples, as shown in Table 3 . The results show that the sensor's accuracy is good and that it has a lot of potential for analyzing biotin in real samples.

In this study, a GQD-encased in MIP was created and used as an efficient fluorescence sensor via surface imprinting technique for biotin identification and determination via the PET method. The specific detection and identification of biotin in biological materials was successfully accomplished using this receptor, which benefited from the high sensitivity of the QD and the high specificity of the MIP. This cost-effective and environmentally friendly optical sensor demonstrated a low detection limit and high accuracy in complicated sample analysis, such as biological sample analysis, indicating a promising future in advanced analytical applications.

Parizad Mohammadnejad: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Seyed Mohamadreza Milani Hosseini: Supervision, Project administration, Conceptualization. Beheshteh Sohrabi: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The support for this investigation by the Research Council of the Iran University of Science and Technology through a grant is gratefully acknowledged.
